grammarLessons.name: 있어요 & 없어요 — There Is / There Isn't

cards.grammarNote:
**집** = the physical house or home.
**집에 가다** (jibe gada) = to go home.
Location particles: **에** for direction/existence, **에서** for actions taking place somewhere.

Korea has some of the world's highest rates of apartment living. '아파트' (apateu) refers to large high-rise apartment complexes (different from the Western sense of 'apartment'). Complexes are often called '단지' (danji).

**학교** (hakgyo) is a Hanja compound: **학** (學) = study + **교** (校) = institution.
Use **에** for destination (going to school): **학교에 가요**.
Use **에서** for where an action takes place: **학교에서 공부해요** (I study at school).
Subject particle: **아이들이** — **이** attaches after a consonant-ending word.

**대학교** (daehakgyo) = **대** (大, great/big) + **학** (學, study) + **교** (校, institution).
**대학교에서 공부해요** — note **에서**, not **에**, because studying is an action happening at the location, not a destination.
The verb **공부하다** (gongbuhada) = to study, follows the **-하다** pattern: noun **공부** + **하다**.

**사무실** (samusil) = **사무** (事務, office work/affairs) + **실** (室, room).
**에서** is used because working is an action happening at the location: **사무실에서 일해요**.
**일하다** (ilhada) = to work — another **-하다** verb. The stem is **일** (work) + **하다**.

**병원** (byeongwon) is a Hanja compound: **병** (病) = illness + **원** (院) = institution/yard.
The suffix **-원** (院) appears in many institutional words: **학원** (hagwon, academy), **고아원** (orphanage).
**에 가요** = going to the hospital (destination). If you were saying you are *at* the hospital, you'd say **병원에 있어요** (I am at the hospital).

**슈퍼마켓** (syupeomaket) is a loanword from English.
**가야 해요** = must go / have to go. This uses the pattern **verb stem + 아야/어야 하다** to express obligation.
**가다** → stem **가** + **야 해요** → **가야 해요** (must go). A very common pattern for necessity.
cards.englishPhrase: I need to go to the supermarket.

cards.grammarNote:
**은행** (eunhaeng) = Hanja **은** (銀, silver) + **행** (行, row/institution) — originally referred to silver exchange institutions.
**에서 왔어요** = came from. **에서** marks the starting point of movement (source), not just the location of action. **왔어요** = came (past tense of **오다**, to come).

**도서관** (doseogwan) = **도서** (圖書, books/documents) + **관** (館, hall/building).
The suffix **-관** appears in many public buildings: **박물관** (museum), **영화관** (cinema), **미술관** (art gallery).
**에서** marks where the action of studying happens: **도서관에서 공부해요** (I study at the library).

**식당** (sikdang) = **식** (食, eat/food) + **당** (堂, hall). A more informal word than **레스토랑** (restaurant, loanword).
**식당이 꽉 찼어요**: **이** is the subject particle (after consonant). **꽉** = completely/tightly. **찼어요** = past tense of **차다** (to be full/filled).

**카페** (kape) is a loanword from French/English. Korea has an extremely dense cafe culture — some cities have more cafes per capita than any country in the world.
**에서 만나요** — **에서** because meeting is an action performed *at* the location. Compare: **카페에 가요** (going to the cafe, destination) vs. **카페에서 만나요** (meeting at the cafe, action location).

**호텔이 시내에 있어요**: the structure is subject + location + **있어요**.
**있어요** (isseoyo) expresses existence and location: X**에 있어요** = X is at/in [place].
**시내** (sinai) = downtown/city center. **이** is the subject particle after the consonant-ending **호텔**.

**공항** (gonghang) = **공** (空, sky/air) + **항** (港, port/harbor).
**여기서** = **여기** (here) + **서** (abbreviated form of **에서**). Short forms **여기서**, **거기서**, **저기서** are common in speech.
**멀어요** = is far (adjective used as predicate, standard in Korean — no need for a copula).
cards.englishPhrase: The airport is far from here.

cards.grammarNote:
**역** (yeok) = train/subway station. The Chinese character is **驛**.
**가까이에 있어요** = is near. **가까이** (nearby) + **에** (location particle) + **있어요** (exists/is).
Station names in Korean just add **역**: **강남역** (Gangnam Station), **서울역** (Seoul Station).
cards.englishPhrase: The station is near the hotel.

cards.grammarNote:
**정류장** (jeongnyujang) = **정류** (停留, stopping/pausing) + **장** (場, place/ground).
**에서 기다리세요** — **에서** because waiting is an action at the location.
**-세요** is the polite imperative: **기다리세요** = please wait. **기다리다** (gidarida) = to wait.

**공원** (gongwon) = **공** (公, public) + **원** (園, garden/park).
**에서 놀아요** — **에서** because playing is an action at the location. Children **play** (verb) in the park, so **에서** is correct.
**아이들이** = children + subject particle **이**. The plural suffix **들** (deul) is optional but common.
cards.englishPhrase: The children play in the park.

cards.grammarNote:
**광장** (gwangjang) = **광** (廣, wide/broad) + **장** (場, place/ground).
**분수가 있어요** = there is a fountain. **있어요** + subject marks existence: **분수가** (fountain + subject particle **가** after vowel).
**광장에** uses **에** for static existence — the fountain simply *is* there.
cards.englishPhrase: There's a fountain in the square.

cards.grammarNote:
**거리** (geori) means both 'street' and 'distance' — two different words written identically.
**이 거리에 있어요** = is on this street. **에** + **있어요** for static location.
**식당이** — subject particle **이** after consonant. The demonstrative **이** (this) comes just before the noun it modifies.
cards.englishPhrase: The restaurant is on this street.

cards.grammarNote:
**횡단보도** (hoengdanbodo) = **횡단** (橫斷, crossing/traversing) + **보도** (步道, walking path).
**에서 건너세요** — **에서** because crossing is an action *at* the crosswalk.
**건너세요** = please cross (polite imperative of **건너다**, to cross).

**교차로** (gyocharo) = **교차** (交叉, crossing/intersecting) + **로** (路, road).
**에서** marks where the turning action happens.
**왼쪽으로** = to the left: **왼쪽** (left side) + **으로** (direction particle, used after consonants). The particle **-(으)로** expresses direction of movement.

**다리** (dari) is a homophone meaning both 'bridge' and 'leg'. Context makes it clear.
**다리를 건넜어요** = crossed the bridge. **를** is the object particle after a vowel-ending word.
**건넜어요** = past tense of **건너다** (to cross). Past tense is formed by adding **았어요/었어요** to the verb stem.

**모퉁이** (motungi) = corner (of a road or building).
**에서 도세요** — **에서** marks where the turning action occurs.
**오른쪽으로** = to the right: **오른쪽** + direction particle **으로** (after consonant). **으로** vs. **로**: use **으로** after consonants (except ㄹ), **로** after vowels or ㄹ.

**시내** (sinai) = city interior/downtown; **시** (市, city) + **내** (內, inside).
**박물관이 시내에 있어요** — location + **있어요** expresses where something is. **박물관** (bakmulgwan) = **박** (博, broad) + **물** (物, things) + **관** (館, hall) — museum.

**동네** (dongne) = neighbourhood/local area. A warm, informal word for one's immediate community.
**조용한 동네에 살아요** — **에 살다** = to live in/at a place. The particle **에** is used with **살다** (to live) — this is an exception where **에** (not **에서**) is used with this specific verb of residence.
cards.englishPhrase: I live in a quiet neighbourhood.

cards.grammarNote:
**해변** (haebyeon) = **해** (海, sea) + **변** (邊, edge/side). Literally 'sea-side'.
**아름다워요** = is beautiful. Korean adjectives function directly as predicates: no need for 'is' + adjective as in English. **아름답다** (areumdapda) → **아름다워요** (irregular ㅂ conjugation).

**산** (san) = mountain. Also the Hanja **山**. Korea is about 70% mountainous — hiking is one of the most popular national pastimes.
**높아요** = is high/tall. Again, the adjective **높다** (nopda) directly functions as the predicate: **산이 높아요** = the mountain is tall. No separate 'to be' verb needed.

**방문하고 싶어요** = want to visit. The pattern **verb stem + -고 싶다** expresses desire: **가고 싶어요** (want to go), **먹고 싶어요** (want to eat), **방문하고 싶어요** (want to visit).
**박물관을** — **을** is the object particle after a consonant-ending word.
cards.englishPhrase: I want to visit the museum.

cards.grammarNote:
**영화관** (yeonghwagwan) = **영화** (映畫, movie/film) + **관** (館, hall/building).
**영화관에 가요** — destination uses **에**: going *to* the cinema.
**오늘 밤** = tonight. Time words in Korean appear at the beginning of the sentence or before the main verb, not at the end as in English.
cards.englishPhrase: We're going to the cinema tonight.

cards.grammarNote:
**지도** (jido) = **지** (地, earth/land) + **도** (圖, diagram/map).
**지도 있어요?** = Do you have a map? In Korean, **있어요** covers both 'to be' (existence) and 'to have' (possession). The subject/topic is often dropped when it is clear from context — here 'you' is implied.

**방향** (banghyang) = **방** (方, direction/side) + **향** (向, facing/toward).
**이에요** is the copula 'is/am/are' after a consonant-ending noun: **방향이에요** = is a direction.
**어느** (eoneu) = which (of known options), used before nouns. Compare **무슨** (museun) = what kind of.

**거리** (geori) meaning 'distance' shares spelling with **거리** (geori) 'street' — context distinguishes them.
**까지** (kkaji) = until/up to: **호텔까지** = to the hotel / as far as the hotel.
**얼마예요?** = how much is it? **얼마** (eolma) = how much, **예요** = is (copula after vowel).
cards.englishPhrase: What's the distance to the hotel?

cards.grammarNote:
Korean verbs come at the end of the sentence.
**가다** (to go) → **가요** in polite present tense.
Destination uses **에**: **슈퍼에 가요** = I'm going to the supermarket.
Opposite direction: **오다** (oda) = to come.

**오다** (oda) = to come (toward the speaker).
**오세요** = please come (polite imperative: verb stem + **-세요**).
**우리 집에** — **우리** (our/my) is commonly used where English says 'my': **우리 집** = my house, **우리 엄마** = my mom. Koreans say 'our' even for things only they possess.

**여행하다** (yeohaenghada) = **여행** (旅行, travel) + **하다** (to do). A **-하다** verb.
**여행하는 걸 좋아해요** = I love traveling. The pattern **verb-는 것/걸 좋아하다** = to like doing something. **것** (geot) is a nominalizer turning a verb into a noun; **걸** is its contracted form used as an object.

**걷다** (geotda) has an irregular ㄷ conjugation: **걷다** → **걸어요** (present), **걸어서** (walking/by walking).
**걸어서** = by walking (means of transport). The particle **에서** on a verb stem can indicate manner/means.
**출근하다** (chulgeunhada) = to commute/go to work. Its counterpart is **퇴근하다** = to leave work.
cards.englishPhrase: I walk to work every day.

cards.grammarNote:
**운전하다** (unjeonhada) = **운전** (運轉, operation/driving) + **하다**. A **-하다** verb.
**까지** (kkaji) = up to/as far as: **사무실까지** = all the way to the office.
**운전해요** → present tense of **운전하다**: **하다** verbs conjugate as **해요** in polite present.

**돌다** (dolda) = to turn/rotate. In directions, the honorific/polite imperative form **도세요** is used.
**왼쪽으로** = to the left: direction particle **-(으)로** after **왼쪽** (left side). Use **으로** after consonants, **로** after vowels.
**모퉁이에서** — **에서** marks where the turning action takes place.

**건너다** (geonneoda) = to cross (a road, bridge, or body of water).
**조심해서** = carefully. **-아서/어서** attached to **조심하다** (to be careful) creates 'doing carefully'.
**길을** — **길** (road/way) + object particle **을**. **건너세요** = please cross (polite imperative).

**따라가다** (ttaragada) = **따라** (following) + **가다** (to go) — a compound verb meaning 'to follow (along)'.
**이 거리를 따라가세요** = follow this street. **거리를** = street + object particle **를**.
**-세요** is the polite imperative suffix, used for polite commands and requests.

**도착하다** (dochaghada) = to arrive/reach. **도착** (到着) = **도** (到, arrive) + **착** (着, attach/reach).
Note: the example phrase uses **어떻게 가요?** (how do I go?) rather than **도착해요**, showing how Korean often uses the more natural phrasing.
**역에** = to the station (destination **에**).
cards.englishPhrase: How do I reach the station?

cards.grammarNote:
**들어가다** (deureogada) = **들어** (entering) + **가다** (to go) — compound verb meaning 'to go in/enter'.
**정문으로** = through/via the main door. **정문** (正門) = main gate/door. **으로** marks the means or direction of entry.
**들어가세요** = please enter (polite imperative).

**나가다** (nagada) = to go out/leave (a place). Compound of **나** (out) + **가다** (go).
Note: the phrase uses **퇴근하다** (toegeunhada) = to leave work specifically, rather than **나가다**.
**다섯 시에** = at five o'clock. Time + **에** = at [time]. Clocks use Sino-Korean numbers: **다섯** is native Korean, but **오 시** (5 o'clock) also appears in formal contexts.

**출발하다** (chulbalhada) = to depart/set off. **출발** (出發) = **출** (出, exit/out) + **발** (發, send off/dispatch).
**기차가** = the train + subject particle **가** (after vowel).
**아홉 시에** = at nine o'clock. Hours use native Korean numbers: **하나, 둘, 셋...** up to twelve for telling time.

**도착하다** (dochaghada) = to arrive. A **-하다** verb; conjugates to **도착해요** in polite present.
**내일 도착해요** = we arrive tomorrow. Time adverbs like **내일** (tomorrow) come before the verb.
Note: both 'to arrive' and 'to reach' use the same Korean word **도착하다**, just as the English words are close in meaning.

**방문하다** (bangmunhada) = to visit. **방문** (訪問) = **방** (訪, call upon) + **문** (問, inquire).
**-고 싶어요** = want to: **방문하고 싶어요** = want to visit. This desire pattern attaches **-고 싶다** to the verb stem.
**박물관을** = museum + object particle **을** (after consonant).
cards.englishPhrase: I want to visit the museum.

cards.grammarNote:
Korean location expressions use postpositions (placed after the noun).
**안에** (inside), **위에** (on top), **아래에** (under)
**옆에** (next to), **앞에** (in front), **뒤에** (behind)
Existence/location: **있어요** (isseoyo).
cards.englishPhrase: The keys are in the bag.

cards.grammarNote:
**위에** (wie) = on top of / above. **위** (上/위, top/above) + **에** (location particle).
The structure is: subject + reference noun + **위에** + **있어요**.
**테이블 위에 있어요** = is on the table. The reference noun (**테이블**, table) comes directly before the location word with no particle.
cards.englishPhrase: The book is on the table.

cards.grammarNote:
**~에** is the location/destination particle.
With **있다** (to be/exist): **사무실에 있어요** = I am at the office (static location).
Contrast with **에서**: **사무실에서 일해요** = I work at the office (action happening there).
Key rule: static existence → **에 있어요**; active verb → **에서 [verb]**.

**아래에** (araee) and **밑에** (mite) both mean 'under/below'. **밑에** is slightly more colloquial.
The phrase uses **밑에** rather than **아래에** — both are correct but **밑에** is more common in everyday speech.
**고양이가** = cat + subject particle **가** (after vowel). **테이블 밑에 있어요** = is under the table.
cards.englishPhrase: The cat is under the table.

cards.grammarNote:
**옆에** (yeope) = next to / beside. **옆** = side/flank.
Structure: subject + reference noun + **옆에** + **있어요**.
**은행이 카페 옆에 있어요** = the bank is next to the cafe. The reference noun (**카페**) comes before the positional word with no particle between them.
cards.englishPhrase: The bank is next to the cafe.

cards.grammarNote:
**사이에** (saie) = between. **사이** = gap/space between two things.
**학교와 도서관 사이에** = between the school and the library. The connector **와/과** (and/with) links the two reference nouns: **와** after vowels, **과** after consonants.
**공원이** = park + subject particle **이** (after consonant).
cards.englishPhrase: The park is between the school and the library.

cards.grammarNote:
**앞에** (ape) = in front of. **앞** = front.
Structure: subject + reference noun + **앞에** + **있어요**.
**택시가 호텔 앞에 있어요** = the taxi is in front of the hotel. **택시가** = taxi + subject particle **가** (after vowel-ending word).
cards.englishPhrase: The taxi is in front of the hotel.

cards.grammarNote:
**뒤에** (dwie) = behind / at the back. **뒤** = back/rear.
Structure: subject + reference noun + **뒤에** + **있어요**.
**정원이** = garden + subject particle **이**. **정원** (庭園) = **정** (庭, courtyard) + **원** (園, garden). The suffix **-원** (園) appears in many garden-related words.
cards.englishPhrase: The garden is behind the house.

cards.grammarNote:
**근처에** (geuncheoe) = nearby/in the vicinity. More commonly used than **가까이에** in questions like this.
**여기 근처에 식당이 있어요?** — note **있어요** used as a question by rising intonation alone (no question word needed).
**있어요** covers both 'there is' and 'do you have' — a highly versatile verb.
cards.englishPhrase: Is there a restaurant near here?

cards.grammarNote:
**멀리** (meolli) = far away (adverb). The adjective form is **멀다** (meolda) = to be far.
**공항이 멀어요?** = Is the airport far? Korean adjectives act as predicates directly: **멀어요** = is far.
Adverb form: **멀리 있어요** (is far away). Adjective predicate: **멀어요** (is far). Both are common.

Korean has a three-way distance system:
**여기** (here — near speaker), **거기** (there — near listener), **저기** (over there — far from both).
This mirrors the demonstrative system **이/그/저** (this/that/that over there).

**저기** (jeogi) = over there (far from both speaker and listener) — note the phrase uses **저기**, not **거기**.
**거기** = there (near the listener). **저기** = over there (far from both). **여기** = here (near the speaker).
These three words correspond to the demonstrative adjectives: **이** (this/near speaker), **그** (that/near listener), **저** (that over there).

**어디에나** (eodienа) = everywhere. **어디** (where) + **에나** (even at, wherever).
The **-나** suffix adds inclusivity: **어디나** = anywhere/everywhere, **언제나** = anytime/always, **누구나** = anyone/everyone.
**관광객이 있어요** = there are tourists. **이** is the subject particle after the consonant-ending **관광객**.

**조용한 곳으로 가요** = let's go to a quiet place. The phrase uses **곳** (place) rather than **어딘가에** (somewhere).
**곳으로** = toward the place: direction particle **으로** after consonant-ending **곳**.
**조용한** = quiet (adjective modifying **곳**). Adjectives precede the nouns they modify, just as in English.

**앉을 자리가 없어요** = there is nowhere to sit. **없어요** (eopseoyo) = there is not / does not exist — the negative of **있어요**.
**앉을** = verb-modifier form of **앉다** (to sit): 'a seat for sitting'. The **-(으)ㄹ** modifier expresses future/potential action on the following noun.
**자리가** = seat + subject particle **가**.

**왼쪽** (oenjjok) = left side. **왼** = left, **쪽** = side/direction.
**왼쪽으로** = to the left. Direction particle **으로** after consonant.
Directional pairs: **왼쪽** (left) ↔ **오른쪽** (right); **앞** (front) ↔ **뒤** (back); **위** (up) ↔ **아래/밑** (down/below).

**오른쪽** (oreunjjok) = right side. **오른** = right, **쪽** = side/direction.
**오른쪽으로** = to the right. Direction particle **으로** after consonant.
Note: **오른쪽** is the standard word; **우측** (右側) is a more formal/Hanja-based synonym seen on signs.

**직진** (jikjin) = **직** (直, straight) + **진** (進, advance/proceed). A Hanja compound.
**직진하세요** = please go straight: **직진** + **하다** + polite imperative **-세요**.
This is the standard instruction for 'go straight' when giving directions — you'll hear it often from navigation apps.

**북쪽** (bukjjok) = north side/direction. **북** (北, north) + **쪽** (side).
Cardinal directions: **북쪽** (north), **남쪽** (south), **동쪽** (east), **서쪽** (west).
**도시는 북쪽에 있어요** — **은** is the topic particle, contrasting 'the city' with other subjects. **에 있어요** = is located at.
cards.englishPhrase: The city is to the north.

cards.grammarNote:
**남쪽** (namjjok) = south side. **남** (南, south) + **쪽** (side).
**해변은 남쪽에 있어요** — topic particle **은** after **해변** (beach, consonant ending).
The topic particle **은/는** often implies contrast or a new topic; the subject particle **이/가** marks the grammatical subject more neutrally.
cards.englishPhrase: The beach is to the south.

cards.grammarNote:
**동쪽** (dongjjok) = east side. **동** (東, east) + **쪽** (side).
**동쪽에서 떠요** — **에서** here marks the source/starting point of movement: the sun rises *from* the east.
This is one use of **에서** as 'from' (source), not just 'action location'. **떠요** = rises (from **뜨다**, to float up/rise).
cards.englishPhrase: The sun rises in the east.

cards.grammarNote:
**서쪽** (seojjok) = west side. **서** (西, west) + **쪽** (side).
**서쪽으로 져요** — direction particle **으로** indicates the direction of setting. **지다** (jida) = to set (of the sun) / to sink.
Contrast: **뜨다** (rise) ↔ **지다** (set) — the pairing for the sun's movement.
cards.englishPhrase: The sun sets in the west.

cards.grammarNote:
**살다** (salda) = to live/reside. Uses **에** (not **에서**) for location: **시내 근처에 살아요** = I live near downtown.
This is a key exception: **살다** takes **에** even though living is an ongoing state, not just a destination.
**근처에** (geuncheoe) = nearby/in the vicinity.
cards.englishPhrase: I live near the city centre.

cards.grammarNote:
**묵다** (mukda) is the more natural verb for staying at a hotel — **머물다** is slightly more formal/literary.
**호텔에 묵고 있어요** = we are staying at the hotel (ongoing action). **-고 있어요** = progressive aspect: 'currently doing'.
**에** is used with **묵다** (like **살다**) for the location of residence.

**기다리다** (gidarida) = to wait.
**여기서** = here + abbreviated **에서** (**서**). Short forms: **여기서** (here), **거기서** (there), **저기서** (over there).
**기다릴게요** = I will wait. The ending **-ㄹ/을게요** expresses a volitional promise or intention about the future: 'I will (do something)'.

**만나다** (mannada) = to meet.
**공원에서 만나요** — **에서** because meeting is an action happening at the location, not a destination.
**만나요** can function as both a statement ('we meet') and a suggestion ('let's meet') depending on context and intonation. Context makes the meaning clear.

**길을 잃다** (gireul ilta) = to lose the road/way. Literally 'to lose the road'.
**-지 마세요** = please don't [verb]. Negative polite imperative: **verb stem + -지 말다** → **-지 마세요** in polite form.
**길을 잃지 마세요** = Don't get lost! **잃다** (ilta) = to lose (an ㅎ-irregular verb in some contexts).

**찾다** (chatda) = to find / to look for (context determines which).
**못 찾겠어요** = can't find. **못** (mot) before a verb = inability: 'cannot [verb]'. **못 가요** (can't go), **못 먹어요** (can't eat).
Compare: **안** (an) = won't/doesn't (volitional); **못** (mot) = can't (inability).

**찾다** (chatda) also means 'to look for / to search for' — the same verb as 'to find'.
**찾고 있어요** = am looking for (progressive). The pattern **verb stem + -고 있어요** = currently doing: **먹고 있어요** (eating), **공부하고 있어요** (studying), **찾고 있어요** (looking for).
**약국을** = pharmacy + object particle **을** (after consonant).

**향하다** (hyanghada) = to face toward / head in a direction. **향** (向) = direction/facing.
**역을 향해 가세요** = head toward the station. **향해** = toward (gerund of **향하다**).
**역을** = station + object particle **을**. Then **향해 가세요** = please go toward.

**계속** (gyesok) = continuously/in a row. Functions as both an adverb and a noun.
**계속 직진하세요** = continue straight ahead: **계속** (adverb 'continuously') + **직진하세요** (please go straight).
**계속하다** (gyesokhada) = to continue. The adverb **계속** appears very often in everyday speech: **계속 말해요** (keep talking), **계속 해요** (keep going).

**멈추다** (meomchuda) = to stop (motion/activity).
**모퉁이에서 멈추세요** — **에서** marks where the stopping action occurs.
**멈추세요** = please stop (polite imperative). Another word for stop is **서다** (seoda), which is often used for vehicles: **차가 섰어요** (the car stopped).

**주차하다** (juchahada) = to park (a vehicle). **주차** (駐車) = **주** (駐, to be stationed) + **차** (車, vehicle).
**주차할 수 있어요?** = can park? The pattern **verb-ㄹ/을 수 있다** = can/is able to. **주차할 수 없어요** = cannot park.
**어디에** = where + **에** (destination/location particle).

**예약하다** (yeyakhada) = to reserve/book. **예약** (豫約) = **예** (豫, in advance) + **약** (約, promise/arrange).
**예약했어요** = reserved (past tense). **하다** verbs form past tense by adding **했어요** (changed from **하였어요**).
**방을** = room + object particle **을** (after consonant).

**체크인하다** (chekeuinhada) = to check in. Loanword **체크인** (check-in) + **하다**.
**세 시에** = at three o'clock. Hours of the day use native Korean numbers: **하나 시** (1 o'clock), **두 시** (2), **세 시** (3), **네 시** (4), etc.
**체크인했어요** = checked in (past tense of a **하다** verb).

**체크아웃** (chekeua-ut) = check-out. Another loanword + **하다** verb.
**열한 시예요** = it is eleven o'clock. **열한** = eleven (native Korean: **열** = ten + **한** = one). Time: native Korean numbers for hours, Sino-Korean for minutes.
**은** is the topic particle here (after consonant **체크아웃**), marking 'check-out' as the topic of discussion.

**근처에** (geuncheoe) = nearby / in the vicinity. **근처** (近處) = **근** (近, close) + **처** (處, place).
**근처에 약국이 있어요?** = Is there a pharmacy nearby? **있어요** covers both 'there is' and 'is there' (question by intonation).
**약국** (yakguk) = pharmacy: **약** (藥, medicine) + **국** (局, bureau/office).

**붐벼요** = is crowded/bustling — from **붐비다** (bumbyida). **복잡해요** would mean 'is complicated/congested'.
Note: the example uses **붐벼요** (physically crowded with people) rather than **복잡해요** (congested/complex).
**시장이** = market + subject particle **이**. Korean adjectives directly predicate: no separate 'is' needed.

**조용한** (joyonghan) = quiet (adjective modifier form of **조용하다**).
Predicate form: **조용해요** (is quiet). Modifier form: **조용한** + noun (quiet neighbourhood).
**더 좋아해요** = like more/prefer: **더** = more + **좋아해요** = like. Simple comparative in Korean: **A보다 B를 더 좋아해요** = prefer B to A.

**길을 잃었어요** = I got lost. Literally 'I lost the road'.
**잃었어요** = lost (past tense of **잃다**, to lose). The **었** past tense marker attaches after stems ending in non-ㅏ/ㅗ vowels.
**길** (gil) = road/way — also used metaphorically: **인생의 길** (the road of life). Getting lost in Korean is always expressed with **길** (road), not a vague 'direction'.
